era_context: The mid-8th century CE continues with the Abbasid Caliphate consolidating power in the Islamic world, the Tang Dynasty approaching internal crisis, and the Frankish kingdom expanding its influence in Western Europe. The Byzantine Empire remains engaged in Iconoclasm and frontier conflicts. This year falls in a period of significant geopolitical realignment, with major powers either consolidating or facing internal challenges.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia and Indian subcontinent (limited contemporary records)

events.title: Pope Stephen II's accession and appeal to the Franks
events.region: Papal States and Frankish Kingdom (Italy and Western Europe)
events.description: Pope Stephen II (r. 752–757) becomes pope and faces immediate threats from Lombard King Aistulf, who is expanding Lombard territory at the expense of Papal lands. Stephen II seeks military assistance from the Frankish kingdom under Pepin III (the Short), initiating diplomatic negotiations that will lead to the Frankish-Papal alliance and Pepin's Italian campaigns (754–756 CE). This moment marks the beginning of a crucial political and military partnership that will reshape Western European politics and establish the Franks as the dominant military power in the region.
key_figures: Pepin III (King of the Franks, r. 751–768)

sources.name: Liber Pontificalis (Book of the Popes), compiled 8th–9th centuries
Annales Regni Francorum (Annals of the Frankish Kingdom), compiled early 9th century
Paul the Deacon, History of the Lombards, compiled late 8th century

events.certainty_note: Pope Stephen II's accession in 752 CE is firmly established in Papal and Frankish sources. The date is consistent across major chronicles.
cross_references: Leads to 754-001 (Pepin III's first Italian campaign)

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate consolidation under al-Saffah
events.description: Caliph al-Saffah (r. 750–754) continues consolidating Abbasid power in the Islamic heartland. The year 752 CE falls in the middle of al-Saffah's reign, a period marked by the suppression of remaining Umayyad loyalists, the establishment of a centralized bureaucracy, and the recruitment of Persian administrative talent. Al-Saffah's reign is relatively brief (750–754 CE), but it establishes the foundations for the more transformative reign of his successor, al-Mansur (754–775 CE).

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings, compiled early 10th century

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Abbasid consolidation under al-Saffah is confirmed, but specific events in 752 CE are not precisely documented.

events.title: Tang Dynasty stability and military organization
events.description: Emperor Xuanzong of Tang continues to govern during a period of relative stability. The empire's administrative apparatus remains strong, and the military system is being restructured with increasing reliance on non-Han commanders in frontier positions. The year 752 CE represents the final years of relative peace before the An Lushan Rebellion (755 CE) destabilizes the empire. Underlying tensions are building, but they are not yet visible in the historical record.

sources.name: Jiu Tangshu (Old History of Tang), compiled 945 CE
Xin Tangshu (New History of Tang), compiled 1060 CE

events.certainty_note: Specific events in 752 CE are not clearly documented in surviving sources. This entry reflects the broader era context.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the sparse documentation for 752 CE means that any events attributed to this year are largely inferred from broader era context rather than specific contemporary sources. Pope Stephen II's accession is well-documented, but the exact chronology of his early diplomatic negotiations with the Franks is less clear; some sources suggest the initial contact occurred in 753 CE rather than 752 CE.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 752 CE is sparse. Most surviving sources are later compilations (8th–10th centuries) that do not provide precise year-by-year detail for this period. The historical record is heavily skewed toward major political transitions (Papal succession, Abbasid consolidation) and long-term trends (Tang military reorganization) rather than specific events. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are absent from the historical record. The surviving sources are shaped by institutional interests (Papal, Frankish, Islamic) and may omit or downplay events that do not fit the narrative of institutional consolidation or religious authority.

graph_edges.note: Pope Stephen II's appeal to the Franks led to Pepin III's first Italian campaign in 754 CE
Al-Saffah's consolidation of Abbasid power led to al-Mansur's accession in 754 CE
